turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Breyer has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 17-765 United States v. Stitt and the consolidated case.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: There is a federal law called the Armed Career Criminal Act which applies to people who are convicted of unlawfully possessing a firearm. Now the Act requires a sentencing judge to impose a minimum 15-year prison term if that firearms offender has three prior convictions for certain crimes which it then lists. Prior convictions that qualify on that list include convictions for burglary, arson, and extortion and we here consider the meaning of the term 'burglary' as it appears in that federal act. Does that word include a conviction under Tennessee law for burglary of a 'structure' such as a mobile home, a trailer, or a tent or a 'vehicle' when that structure or vehicle is 'designed or adapted for the overnight accommodation of persons'? Does it include a conviction under Arkansas law for burglary of a 'vehicle, building or other structure' that is 'customarily used for overnight accommodation of a person'? We conclude that the federal word in this federal statute 'burglary' does include crimes that fall within either of these state definitions. We base our analysis upon an earlier case, Taylor v. United States. In Taylor we held that the Act, the Armed Career Criminal Act, requires us to evaluate a prior state conviction in terms of how the state law defines the offense not in terms of how an individual offender committed the offense on a particular occasion. So we look to the definition of the offense and we held that Congress intended the word in this federal statute 'burglary' to include all state crimes that meet the description of what we called, in that opinion, generic burglary. And we said about generic burglary that it includes at least the common-law definition, at least, namely breaking and entering a dwelling at night with intent to commit a felony, but we added it has to include more. That we thought is because limiting the definition to dwellings would have 'little relevance to modern law enforcement concerns.' We made clear that the definition must reflect instead 'the generic sense in which the term was used in the criminal codes of most states' and given the Act's purpose it must include some ordinary run-of-the-mill burglaries and not just aggravated burglaries. We held that the elements of generic burglary under the federal act are 'an unlawful or unprivileged entry into or remaining in a building or other structure with intent to commit a crime.' We now conclude that the relevant language in both the Tennessee and Arkansas statute falls within the scope of generic burglary as Taylor defined it for one thing when Congress included the word 'burglary' in the statute in 1986 a majority of state burglary statutes did cover vehicles adapted or customarily used for lodging, either explicitly or defining building or structure to include those vehicles. And Congress as we said in Taylor saw word burglary as an inherently dangerous crime because burglary 'creates the possibility of a violent confrontation between the burglar and an occupant or someone who comes to investigate.' An offender who breaks into a mobile home, an RV, a camping tent or an another vehicle or structure that is adapted or customarily used for lodging runs a similar and sometimes a greater risk of violent confrontation. Respondent replies that some of our prior decisions including Taylor and Mathis described certain vehicles as falling outside the scope generic burglary, but in those cases we did not consider the question presented here, whether statutes that prohibit burglary of structures or vehicles that are designed, adapted or customarily used for overnight accommodation qualifies as generic burglary and as we said we now address that question and we hold that they do. There are several other arguments to the contrary. We do not find them strong enough to outweigh the factors we just mentioned and we discuss all these matters further in our opinion. For these reasons we reverse a contrary judgment of the Sixth Circuit Court of Appeals in this Stitt case and we remand to the Eighth Circuit in Sims so that it can consider if appropriate a different part of the Arkansas burglary statute that we have not discussed yet covering a vehicle "where any person lives" that has not been considered by the lower courts.
